pois.commentary: The Sugarloaf Islands off New Plymouth host NZ fur seal colonies, little blue penguin nesting sites, and diverse seabird breeding colonies including shag, petrel, and tern species. Kayak tours provide the best viewing. The islands are visible from the Coastal Walkway.

reviews.summary: Great wildlife close to town. Kayak tours are the best way to see them. Seals are reliable.

pois.name: Tapuae Marine Reserve
pois.short: Marine reserve around the Sugarloaf Islands — protected reef systems with recovering fish populations.
pois.commentary: Tapuae Marine Reserve protects the waters around the Sugarloaf Islands and the adjacent coast. Reef fish populations are recovering, with blue cod, butterfish, and crayfish increasing. Snorkelling and diving reveal kelp forests and diverse invertebrate communities.

reviews.summary: Good snorkelling in protected waters. Fish life is increasing since reserve status. Best in calm conditions.

pois.name: Egmont National Park — Whio Habitat
pois.short: Mt Taranaki's rivers support endangered whio (blue duck) — one of the North Island's key populations.
pois.commentary: The rivers draining Mt Taranaki provide important habitat for whio (blue duck), NZ's rarest waterfowl. DOC's whio recovery programme includes stoat trapping and river monitoring. The Stony River and Waiwhakaiho are known whio sites. Patient scanning of river margins may be rewarded.

reviews.summary: Beautiful river walks. Whio sightings require patience and luck. The mountain streams are pristine.

pois.name: Barrett Lagoon — Wetland Walk
pois.short: Coastal lagoon near New Plymouth with black swan, pied stilt, and seasonal wading birds.
pois.commentary: Barrett Lagoon on the New Plymouth coast is an accessible wetland for bird viewing. Black swan, pied stilt, grey teal, and paradise shelduck are resident. Seasonal waders including godwit visit during migration. The lagoon connects to the New Plymouth Coastal Walkway.

reviews.summary: Easy birding on the Coastal Walkway. Good variety of waterbirds. Pleasant in any weather.

pois.name: Pukekura Park — Bush Birds & Eels
pois.short: Heritage park in central New Plymouth with excellent bush birds, eels in the lakes, and native plantings.
pois.commentary: Pukekura Park in the heart of New Plymouth features mature native bush with excellent populations of tui, kereru, fantail, and grey warbler. The park lakes hold longfin eels and native fish. The waterfall and fern house add botanical interest.

reviews.summary: Lovely urban park with good bush birds. The eels in the lake are fascinating. Free and central.

pois.name: Ōākura Beach — Rock Pool Ecology
pois.short: Rich rock pools and reef flats — sea anemones, starfish, and crabs at low tide.
pois.commentary: The rock platforms at Ōākura Beach reveal rich intertidal ecology at low tide. Sea anemones, cushion stars, hermit crabs, and chitons inhabit the pools, while the reef flats support diverse algae communities. An excellent family-friendly marine education site.

reviews.summary: Great for kids. The rock pools are teeming with life at low tide. Easy access from the car park.

pois.name: Lake Mangamahoe — Forest Birds & Waterfowl
pois.short: Scenic lake with Mt Taranaki reflection — NZ scaup, dabchick, and bush birds in the surrounding forest.
pois.commentary: Lake Mangamahoe near Inglewood offers the classic Mt Taranaki reflection photo. The lake supports NZ scaup, NZ dabchick, and grey teal. The surrounding native bush holds tui, bellbird, kereru, and fantail. A loop walk circles the lake through the forest.

reviews.summary: Beautiful scenery. Good bird variety. The Taranaki reflection is iconic on a calm morning.

pois.name: Cape Egmont — Seabird Coast
pois.short: Taranaki's western cape with seabird viewing — gannets, shearwaters, and petrels offshore.
pois.commentary: Cape Egmont on Taranaki's western coast offers good seabird viewing, particularly during strong westerly winds that push pelagic birds close to shore. Australasian gannet, flesh-footed shearwater, and various petrel species are regular. The lighthouse adds scenic interest.

reviews.summary: Good seabird viewing in the right conditions. The lighthouse is photogenic. Can be very windy.

pois.name: Govett-Brewster Art Gallery / Len Lye Centre
pois.short: Bold contemporary art gallery featuring kinetic artist Len Lye's mesmerising sculptures in a striking mirror-steel building.
pois.commentary: The Govett-Brewster Art Gallery / Len Lye Centre is one of New Zealand's most significant contemporary art institutions, located in New Plymouth. The gallery is home to the Len Lye Collection — the world's largest collection of works by the pioneering New Zealand-born kinetic sculptor and experimental filmmaker Len Lye, whose mesmerising moving steel sculptures are unlike anything else in the art world. The purpose-built Len Lye Centre, opened in 2015, is a stunning building clad in reflective stainless steel that mirrors the sky and street — an artwork in itself. Inside, Lye's towering kinetic sculptures are activated at regular intervals, creating dramatic performances of movement and sound. The adjacent Govett-Brewster gallery presents a bold programme of contemporary New Zealand and international art. Admission is free. The gallery has punched well above its weight since opening in 1970, establishing New Plymouth as an unlikely contemporary art destination. Allow two hours to see both spaces and catch a sculpture activation. Check the daily schedule for activation times.

reviews.summary: The Len Lye sculptures are absolutely mesmerising — watching them activate is a jaw-dropping experience. The building is stunning and the wider gallery programme is consistently excellent. A genuine world-class art experience in New Plymouth.

pois.name: Back Beach / Ngā Motu New Plymouth
pois.short: Wild surf beach with dramatic Sugar Loaf Islands offshore and stunning Taranaki mountain views.
pois.commentary: Back Beach (also known as Ngā Motu Beach) is New Plymouth's wild western beach, stretching beneath the dramatic volcanic remnants of the Sugar Loaf Islands (Ngā Motu) — a chain of rocky islets that are the eroded remains of an ancient volcano. The beach offers powerful surf breaks popular with experienced surfers, though conditions can be challenging. The setting is spectacular — volcanic rock formations, the Sugar Loaf Islands rising from the sea, and on clear days, the perfect cone of Mount Taranaki towering behind the city. The Paritutu Rock track nearby provides a steep but rewarding climb to a summit with extraordinary 360-degree views. The Sugar Loaf Islands are a marine protected area home to fur seals, little blue penguins, and rare reef fish. Kayak tours to the islands run in summer. The Coastal Walkway connects Back Beach to the city centre along the waterfront. This is Taranaki's most dramatic coastal landscape, combining raw natural beauty with accessible adventure.

reviews.summary: Dramatic and beautiful — the Sugar Loaf Islands are stunning and the surf is powerful. Climb Paritutu Rock for unforgettable views of the coast and mountain. The Coastal Walkway connection is brilliant.

pois.name: Fitzroy Beach New Plymouth
pois.short: Popular New Plymouth surf and swimming beach with a vibrant local atmosphere and mountain backdrop.
pois.commentary: Fitzroy Beach is one of New Plymouth's most popular beaches, a stretch of dark volcanic sand (characteristic of the Taranaki coast) with reliable surf breaks and a strong local beach culture. The beach is patrolled during summer and offers good swimming conditions between the flags, while surfers spread along the beach to catch the various breaks. The surf club is a community hub, and the beach has a vibrant, family-friendly atmosphere on summer weekends. Fitzroy is connected to the city centre via the celebrated New Plymouth Coastal Walkway, a 13-kilometre shared path that is one of the best urban coastal walks in New Zealand. The dark sand, crashing waves, and views of Mount Taranaki on clear days create a distinctively Taranaki beach experience. The East End Reserve behind the beach offers shade and picnic facilities. Fitzroy is easy to reach from central New Plymouth and has good parking. The waves are generally suitable for intermediate surfers, with surf schools operating for beginners.

reviews.summary: A great surf and swimming beach with a real community feel. The dark volcanic sand is unique and the mountain views on clear days are stunning. The Coastal Walkway connection is a huge bonus.

pois.name: Mike's Organic Brewery
pois.short: New Zealand's first certified organic brewery, producing natural beers in a rural Urenui setting.
pois.commentary: Mike's Organic Brewery holds the distinction of being New Zealand's first certified organic brewery, established by Mike Neilson in the small coastal settlement of Urenui, north of New Plymouth. The brewery produces a range of beers using only certified organic ingredients, from malt and hops to the yeast, resulting in clean, natural flavours without artificial additives. The range includes a refreshing Organic Pale Ale, a robust Porter, and seasonal specials. The brewery and tasting room occupy a rustic rural property where visitors can sample the range and learn about organic brewing practices. Mike's passion for sustainability extends to the brewery operations, with waste grain going to local farms and minimal packaging. The location is about 30 minutes north of New Plymouth on SH3, easily combined with a visit to the Three Sisters rock formations or the White Cliffs walkway nearby. Phone ahead to confirm opening hours.

reviews.summary: A genuine, small-scale brewing operation with beers that taste authentically different. Mike is a passionate host who loves sharing his organic philosophy. The rural setting adds to the charm.

pois.name: Forgotten World Highway
pois.short: New Zealand's most remote heritage highway, 150km through ghost towns, tunnels, and dramatic hill country.
pois.commentary: The Forgotten World Highway (SH43) between Stratford and Taumarunui is New Zealand's loneliest highway, winding 150 kilometres through some of the North Island's most rugged and sparsely populated hill country. The road passes through the Tangarakau Gorge, one of New Zealand's deepest road gorges, the Moki Tunnel blasted through solid rock, and the ghost town of Whangamomona, a self-declared republic since 1989 that holds biennial Republic Day celebrations. Whangamomona's historic hotel offers refreshments and the chance to get your passport stamped. The highway traverses land once heavily milled for timber and farmed, now largely returned to regenerating bush. No fuel is available between Stratford and Taumarunui, so fill up before departing. The road includes some unsealed sections and is narrow and winding throughout. Allow at least four hours for the drive with stops. The sense of remoteness and the dramatic landscape make this one of New Zealand's most memorable drives.

reviews.summary: A truly unique New Zealand road trip through dramatically remote country. Whangamomona is a delightful eccentric stop. The Tangarakau Gorge is spectacular. Ensure a full tank of fuel before starting.

pois.name: Cape Egmont Boat Club & Coastline
pois.short: Dramatic volcanic coastline with rock pools, surf breaks, and spectacular sunsets on Taranaki's western shore.
pois.commentary: The coastline around Cape Egmont on Taranaki's western shore offers some of the region's most dramatic coastal scenery. Dark volcanic rock platforms extend into the Tasman Sea, creating tide pools teeming with marine life and forming the base for powerful surf breaks. The area around the boat ramp is a favourite spot for sunset watching, with the sun dropping into the sea framed by the rugged coastline. On clear days, Mt Taranaki looms behind, creating an iconic combination of mountain and sea. The coastline is part of the Surf Highway 45 route between New Plymouth and Hawera. Rock-pooling at low tide reveals starfish, anemones, and small fish in the volcanic pools. The area is exposed and can be dangerous in rough seas, so stay well back from the waterline during storms. Several Taranaki surf breaks are accessible from nearby pulloffs along the highway.

reviews.summary: Stunning volcanic coastline with incredible sunset views. The combination of dramatic rocks and Mt Taranaki backdrop is uniquely Taranaki. Great for photography and rock-pooling at low tide.

pois.name: Pouakai Tarn Track
pois.short: Iconic alpine track to a reflective mountain tarn providing New Zealand's most photographed volcano view.
pois.commentary: The Pouakai Tarn track is one of New Zealand's most rewarding day walks, leading to a small alpine tarn that provides the most iconic reflection photograph of Mt Taranaki. The walk from the Mangorei Road end takes about 2.5 hours each way through goblin forest draped in moss and lichen, crossing the otherworldly Ahukawakawa Swamp on boardwalks before climbing to the tarn at 1,100 metres. On a calm day, Mt Taranaki is reflected perfectly in the tarn's still surface, creating a photograph that has become one of New Zealand's most recognisable images. The track is rated as moderate, with some steep sections and exposed terrain above the bushline. Weather conditions can change rapidly on the mountain, so carry warm and waterproof layers regardless of the forecast. The walk can also be done as part of the multi-day Pouakai Circuit. The swamp crossing is fascinating in its own right, with rare alpine plant species. Check DOC conditions before departing.

reviews.summary: A stunning walk rewarded with one of New Zealand's most iconic viewpoints. The goblin forest is magical and the tarn reflection photograph is extraordinary on a calm day. Weather dependent, so plan flexible timing.

pois.name: Mike's Organic Brewery
pois.short: New Zealand's only certified organic brewery, in Urenui near New Plymouth.
pois.commentary: Mike's Organic Brewery in Urenui holds the distinction of being New Zealand's only fully certified organic brewery. Founded by Mike Johnson, the brewery produces a range of beers using certified organic malt, hops, and yeast, with the water drawn from a local aquifer. The brewery's Organic Pilsner and Pale Ale have won numerous awards. The cellar door is open for tastings and sales, and the relaxed atmosphere reflects the brewery's ethos of quality over quantity. Urenui is a small coastal settlement about thirty minutes north of New Plymouth on State Highway 3, and the brewery makes an interesting stop on a coastal drive. The surrounding area offers surf beaches and bush walks. Group tours can be arranged by appointment.

reviews.summary: Unique organic beers that taste great. The small-scale operation has genuine character. Worth the detour from New Plymouth.

pois.name: Govett-Brewster Art Gallery / Len Lye Centre
pois.short: NZ's first and only museum of contemporary art — stunning stainless-steel Len Lye Centre with kinetic sculptures.
pois.commentary: The Govett-Brewster Art Gallery, founded in 1970, was NZ's first dedicated contemporary art museum. The Len Lye Centre was added in 2015.

reviews.summary: The Len Lye kinetic sculptures are mesmerising. The architecture is stunning. One of NZ's best art institutions.

pois.name: Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Protected Area
pois.short: Volcanic rock stacks offshore from New Plymouth — seals, penguins, and diving.
pois.commentary: The Sugar Loaf Islands (Ngā Motu) are ancient volcanic rock stacks forming a marine protected area with fur seals and penguins.

reviews.summary: Kayaking among the islands is wonderful. Seal encounters are common. Dramatic volcanic geology.

pois.name: Surf Highway 45 / Coastal Arts Trail
pois.short: Scenic drive along Surf Highway with art galleries, studios, and surf breaks.
pois.commentary: Surf Highway 45 follows the Taranaki coast from New Plymouth to Hāwera, passing surf breaks and artist studios.

reviews.summary: A great way to explore the Taranaki coast. The art studios are genuinely interesting.

pois.name: Aotea Utanganui — Museum of South Taranaki
pois.short: Modern museum in Pātea with rich geological, art, and social history of South Taranaki.
pois.commentary: Aotea Utanganui in Pātea tells the story of South Taranaki through geology, social history, and art.

reviews.summary: A well-curated small museum with surprising depth. The Pātea Māori Club exhibition is a highlight.

pois.name: Tūpare Heritage Garden
pois.short: Arts and Crafts style house and garden designed by James Chapman-Taylor — free heritage gem.
pois.commentary: Tūpare is a heritage garden and house designed by James Chapman-Taylor in his Arts and Crafts style.

reviews.summary: A beautiful free garden with a stunning house. Peaceful riverside setting.

pois.name: Pukeiti Rhododendron Garden
pois.short: World-class rhododendron collection set in 360ha of rainforest on the slopes of Mt Taranaki.
pois.commentary: Pukeiti is a 360-hectare garden on the slopes of Mount Taranaki, home to one of the world's largest collections of rhododendrons.

reviews.summary: Spectacular in spring when the rhododendrons bloom. The rainforest setting is magical. Free entry.

pois.name: Len Lye Wind Wand
pois.short: A 48m kinetic sculpture swaying on the waterfront — Len Lye's dramatic public artwork.
pois.commentary: The Wind Wand is a 48-metre kinetic sculpture on the New Plymouth waterfront, designed by Len Lye.

reviews.summary: Mesmerising to watch — especially in strong wind. Beautiful when lit up at night.

pois.name: Lake Mangamahoe
pois.short: A tranquil lake offering postcard-perfect reflections of Mt Taranaki.
pois.commentary: Lake Mangamahoe is a small man-made lake that on still mornings perfectly reflects Mt Taranaki.

reviews.summary: The reflection of Mt Taranaki on a still morning is picture-perfect. Easy lakeside walk.

pois.name: Parihaka
pois.short: The sacred village where Te Whiti o Rongomai and Tohu Kākahi led a non-violent resistance movement decades before Gandhi.
pois.commentary: Parihaka is one of the most significant sites in New Zealand history. In the 1870s and 1880s, the prophets Te Whiti o Rongomai and Tohu Kākahi led a peaceful resistance against the confiscation of Māori land, instructing followers to plough confiscated land and pull out survey pegs without violence. On 5 November 1881, 1,600 Armed Constabulary troops invaded the unresisting village. The community was broken up and the leaders imprisoned without trial. Parihaka remains a living Māori community and hosts an annual Peace Festival each November. Visitors are welcome but should respect that this is a marae-based community — guided visits can be arranged.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the experience as profoundly moving. The peaceful setting belies the gravity of what happened here. Guided tours provide essential context that self-guided visits cannot.

pois.name: Pouakai Tarns
pois.short: The small alpine tarns that produce NZ’s most famous mountain reflection photograph — Taranaki mirrored in still water.
pois.commentary: The Pouakai Tarns are small alpine pools on the Pouakai Range that, on a calm day, produce a perfect mirror image of Mount Taranaki — arguably the single most photographed scene in New Zealand. The return hike from Mangorei Road end takes 5–7 hours (14 km return, 740 m elevation gain). The track passes through goblin forest thick with moss before emerging onto the open tussock tops. Timing matters — early morning offers the best chance of still water and clear skies. The Pouakai Hut nearby allows an overnight trip for sunrise attempts.

reviews.summary: Photographers and hikers rate this as a must-do. The reflection shot requires luck with weather but the walk itself is superb. The goblin forest section is atmospheric even in cloud. Mud is a constant companion.

pois.name: Pukekura Park
pois.short: A 52-hectare Garden of National Significance in the heart of New Plymouth — lakes, fernery, bush walks, and one of the world’s most scenic cricket grounds.
pois.commentary: Pukekura Park began as a bush reserve in 1876 and has been developed into one of New Zealand’s finest parks. The fernery and display houses are lush year-round, two lakes provide boating and waterfall features, and the bush walks wind through native forest. The park’s cricket ground — with Mount Taranaki as its backdrop — is regularly named among the world’s most beautiful sporting venues. The annual Festival of Lights (December–February) transforms the park with illuminated displays. Free entry, open dawn to dusk.

reviews.summary: Locals and tourists alike love this park. The fernery is a standout, and the Festival of Lights is magical. Some wish the cricket ground had more regular fixtures. Free entry is appreciated.

pois.name: Tūpare Garden
pois.short: An Arts and Crafts–style garden surrounding a 1927 Chapman-Taylor house — one of the Taranaki Garden Trust’s six heritage properties.
pois.commentary: Tūpare was the home of Sir Russell and Lady Matthews, built in 1927 by architect James Chapman-Taylor using his distinctive rammed-earth technique. The 3.6-hectare garden blends formal planting with native bush along the Waiwhakaiho River, and is managed as one of six heritage gardens by the Taranaki Regional Council. Spring rhododendrons and autumn colour are highlights, but the native bush walk is lovely year-round. Free entry, open daily.

reviews.summary: Garden enthusiasts love the blend of formal and native planting. The Chapman-Taylor house is architecturally significant. A peaceful escape close to New Plymouth.

pois.name: Paritutu Rock
pois.short: A dramatic 155-metre volcanic plug on the New Plymouth waterfront requiring a hands-and-feet scramble to the summit, rewarded with 360-degree views across the Tasman Sea.
pois.commentary: Paritutu, meaning 'steep-sided rock' in Maori, is a rugged volcanic spine rising from the New Plymouth industrial port area that demands a short but steep hands-and-feet scramble to its 155-metre summit. The final section involves pulling yourself up fixed chains on exposed rock faces, but the reward is a panoramic view extending across the Tasman Sea toward the offshore islands of Nga Motu (Sugar Loaves), along the Taranaki coast in both directions, and inland to the cone of Taranaki Maunga. The rock is part of the Sugarloaf Islands Marine Park and can be combined with a visit to the Sugar Loaf Island boat tours that depart from nearby Ngamotu Beach. The climb is accessible to most reasonably fit adults.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the accessible thrill of the chain-assisted summit scramble and the disproportionate quality of the views earned for such a short effort. The Sugar Loaf Islands visible from the top make the marine park connection vivid.

pois.name: Surf Highway 45 Coastal Drive
pois.short: A 105-kilometre coastal highway along the Taranaki Bight connecting New Plymouth to Hawera through surf beaches, fishing villages, and black volcanic sand coastline.
pois.commentary: State Highway 45 hugs the Taranaki coast between New Plymouth and Hawera, passing through a string of small surf towns including Oakura, Ōkato, Opunake, and Manaia. The highway earns its 'Surf Highway' name from the consistent left and right-hand breaks that attract surfers from across New Zealand, with Fitzroy Beach in New Plymouth and Opunake Beach in the middle of the highway being the most celebrated breaks. The black volcanic sand beaches are a distinctive Taranaki feature, and the drive provides constant views of Taranaki Maunga rising from the coastal plain with nothing to obstruct the sightlines. The journey works well combined with stops at Cape Egmont Lighthouse and the Tawhiti Museum in Hawera.

reviews.summary: Surfers and coastal drivers alike appreciate Surf Highway 45 as one of New Zealand's most scenic and characterful driving routes, combining consistent surf, beautiful beaches, and the omnipresent backdrop of Taranaki Maunga.

pois.name: Govett-Brewster Art Gallery
pois.short: New Zealand's foremost regional contemporary art museum in New Plymouth, sharing a striking building with the Len Lye Centre and housing a significant national collection.
pois.commentary: The Govett-Brewster Art Gallery has held a leading position in New Zealand's contemporary art landscape since 1970, presenting challenging and internationally significant exhibitions alongside a permanent collection of New Zealand modern art. The gallery underwent a major expansion in 2015 with the addition of the adjacent Len Lye Centre, a stainless-steel clad building housing the world's largest collection of works by kinetic artist Len Lye, whose motorised sculptures and experimental films are housed in a specially designed purpose-built space. The two buildings together form the most architecturally ambitious cultural facility in provincial New Zealand. Entry to the permanent collection is free, making it highly accessible.

reviews.summary: Art enthusiasts frequently cite the Govett-Brewster and its Len Lye collection as a genuine surprise in provincial New Zealand — a world-class contemporary art experience that rewards both casual and serious gallery visitors.

pois.name: Pukekura Park and Brooklands
pois.short: A magnificent 52-hectare Garden of National Significance in central New Plymouth, featuring two boating lakes, ferneries, and the iconic Brooklands open-air amphitheatre.
pois.commentary: Pukekura Park is widely regarded as one of the most beautiful public gardens in New Zealand, a living heritage landscape developed from 1876 and designated a Garden of National Significance. Two large lakes sit at the heart of the park, spanned by an arched bridge and surrounded by sweeping lawns, specimen trees, and native plantings. The adjoining Brooklands open-air concert bowl is one of New Zealand's most celebrated outdoor performance venues, set within a natural amphitheatre surrounded by 100-year-old trees. The fernery and display houses contain New Zealand's finest public collection of subtropical ferns and orchids. During the annual Festival of Lights in December and January, the park is illuminated with elaborate light installations that attract tens of thousands of visitors.

reviews.summary: Pukekura Park is consistently described as a world-class public garden deserving far greater recognition beyond Taranaki. The boating lakes, fernery, and Brooklands amphitheatre each merit a visit in their own right.

pois.name: Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Park Seal Colony
pois.short: New Plymouth's offshore volcanic island group providing boat tour access to New Zealand fur seal colonies, seabird nesting grounds, and the country's smallest marine park.
pois.commentary: The Sugar Loaf Islands — Nga Motu in Maori — are a cluster of volcanic plugs and reefs 1-3 kilometres offshore from New Plymouth's Back Beach, formed from the same geological event that created Paritutu Rock on shore. The islands constitute a marine protected area established in 1986, and boat tours operate from Ngamotu Beach to allow close approach to the sea stacks where New Zealand fur seals haul out and breed. Spotted shags, variable oystercatchers, and white-fronted terns nest on the islands' ledges, and the boat journey provides excellent views of Taranaki Maunga from sea level. The clear waters around the islands are popular for scuba diving and snorkelling, with healthy kelp forests and abundant fish life beneath the surface.

reviews.summary: The combination of New Plymouth's scenic waterfront, volcanic island geology, and accessible seal viewing makes the Sugar Loaf boat tours a compact and rewarding experience. The view of Taranaki Maunga from sea is unique.

pois.name: Whitecliffs Walkway / Te Horo Tunnel
pois.short: Dramatic coastal walk past white papa cliffs and through a hand-hewn pioneer tunnel.
pois.commentary: The Whitecliffs Walkway follows the North Taranaki coastline past towering white papa mudstone cliffs that give the area its name, then descends to the beach to pass through Te Horo Tunnel — a 100-metre passage hand-hewn through solid rock by settlers in the 1870s to connect isolated farms. The Three Sisters rock stacks standing offshore are another highlight. The walk involves beach sections that are only passable at low tide, so checking tide tables is essential. The full circuit is around 10 kilometres and takes 4-5 hours. The cliffs glow white-gold in afternoon light, making this one of Taranaki's most photogenic walks. Sturdy footwear is needed for the rocky beach sections.

reviews.summary: The tunnel is an extraordinary piece of pioneer engineering. The cliff scenery is stunning but tide-dependent access requires planning.

pois.name: Cape Egmont Lighthouse
pois.short: Photogenic cast-iron lighthouse on Taranaki's western coast since 1881.
pois.commentary: Cape Egmont Lighthouse has guided ships around the Taranaki coast since 1881, though it was originally erected at Mana Island near Wellington in 1865 before being relocated. The distinctive red-and-white cast-iron tower stands 12 metres tall on a grassy headland with sweeping views of the Tasman Sea and, on clear days, a dramatic backdrop of Mount Taranaki. The lighthouse is one of New Zealand's most photographed heritage structures, particularly at sunset when the iron glows golden-red. The surrounding reserve has a short loop walk and picnic area. The lighthouse is not open to climb but the exterior and setting make it well worth the detour off the Surf Highway.

reviews.summary: Beautifully maintained lighthouse in a stunning coastal setting. Best visited at sunset for photography. Quick but memorable stop.

pois.name: New Plymouth Coastal Walkway / Te Rewa Rewa Bridge
pois.short: 13km waterfront walkway with iconic whale-bone bridge and Taranaki Maunga views.
pois.commentary: The New Plymouth Coastal Walkway is a 13-kilometre shared path tracing the city's entire waterfront from the Port to Bell Block, consistently rated one of New Zealand's finest urban walks. The walkway's signature landmark is the Te Rewa Rewa Bridge, a striking whale-bone-inspired structure that frames Mount Taranaki through its curving ribs — one of the most photographed structures in the country. Along the route, the Len Lye Wind Wand kinetic sculpture sways 45 metres above the waterfront, playground areas and pools dot the promenade, and the surf breaks of Fitzroy and East End beaches are within steps. The walkway is flat and sealed, suitable for walking, cycling, scootering, and wheelchair access throughout.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's best urban walkways. Te Rewa Rewa Bridge is iconic. Sunset with Taranaki Maunga in silhouette is pure magic.

pois.name: Opunake Walkway & Beach
pois.short: Clifftop walkway with stunning coastal views and sheltered swimming beach.
pois.commentary: The Opunake Walkway follows the clifftop above this small Taranaki coastal town, offering panoramic views along the coast to Cape Egmont and back toward Mount Taranaki. The loop walk takes about an hour and passes through bush, farmland, and along dramatic cliff edges with views down to surf-battered rock platforms. Opunake Beach itself is one of Taranaki's best swimming spots, sheltered by headlands and with a distinctive artificial reef structure that creates a safe swimming area. The town has a growing cafe scene and the new Te Namu community pool complex. Opunake sits midway along the Surf Highway and has been experiencing a creative revival with art galleries and community events. The town makes a pleasant base for exploring the southern Taranaki coast.

reviews.summary: Lovely coastal walk with excellent views. The beach swimming is safe and pleasant. The town has genuine character.

pois.name: Stratford / Shakespeare Town
pois.short: Small town with Shakespeare-themed streets and a glockenspiel clock tower.
pois.commentary: Stratford is a small Taranaki town that has embraced its Shakespearean namesake with enthusiasm. Streets are named after Shakespeare's characters, and the Tudor-style glockenspiel clock tower on the main street performs a Romeo and Juliet scene with mechanical figures four times daily. The town sits at the western end of the Forgotten World Highway and at the northern base of Mount Taranaki, making it a gateway to both attractions. The Pioneer Village museum recreates an 1890s Taranaki settlement. The TET Multisport Hub provides excellent cycling and walking tracks. Stratford is an unpretentious working town with good cafes and a charming community that takes its Shakespeare connection just seriously enough.

reviews.summary: The glockenspiel is charming and the Shakespeare theme adds fun. Good base for Forgotten World Highway or Mount Taranaki.

pois.name: Tongaporutu Three Sisters & Elephant Rock
pois.short: Dramatic eroding sea stacks and elephant-shaped rock accessible only at low tide.
pois.commentary: The Tongaporutu coastline north of New Plymouth features some of New Zealand's most dramatic coastal formations — towering white cliffs, sea stacks known as the Three Sisters, and the famous Elephant Rock (though its trunk collapsed in 2016). The area can only be safely explored at low tide, making timing essential. The walk from Tongaporutu Beach crosses dark volcanic sand to reach the white cliffs, where caves, arches, and pillars have been carved by the sea. This is one of the fastest-eroding coastlines in New Zealand, meaning the landscape changes with every storm. Photographers particularly prize the area for its otherworldly quality. Check tide tables carefully — being caught by an incoming tide against these cliffs is genuinely dangerous.

reviews.summary: Extraordinary coastal scenery that feels prehistoric. Time your visit with the tides. The erosion means every visit is unique.

pois.name: Normanby Dam Dropping
pois.short: Ride a kayak or water sledge over an 8-metre dam drop — a unique Taranaki adrenaline hit.
pois.commentary: Dam dropping at Normanby is one of New Zealand's hidden adrenaline gems — participants ride a kayak or water sledge over the 8-metre Normanby Dam on the Waingongoro River. The activity is run by local operators who provide all equipment and instruction. After a short paddle upstream, you line up at the lip of the dam and drop over the edge into the churning pool below. It's a short but intense burst of excitement, and something you genuinely cannot do anywhere else in the country. The surrounding farmland and small-town setting make it feel refreshingly un-commercial compared to Queenstown's adventure scene.

reviews.summary: Short but absolutely thrilling. The local, low-key vibe makes it special. Not for the faint-hearted but incredibly fun.

pois.name: Pukeiti Rhododendron Garden
pois.short: 360 hectares of rhododendrons planted within native rainforest on Mt Taranaki's slopes.
pois.commentary: Pukeiti is a botanical wonder — 360 hectares of heritage rhododendron gardens threaded through lush native rainforest on the lower slopes of Mount Taranaki. Established in 1951 by enthusiasts who planted rhododendrons among the native bush, the gardens now contain one of the world's most significant collections, with peak flowering from September to November painting the forest in spectacular colour. The Rainforest Walk takes visitors through towering rimu, rata, and kamahi trees festooned with epiphytes, while the Hatcher Garden Pavilion offers views across the canopy to the mountain. Entry is free, managed by the Taranaki Regional Council. Even outside flowering season, the combination of exotic plantings and wild native forest creates an extraordinary landscape.

reviews.summary: A magical fusion of exotic gardens and wild native forest. Peak rhododendron season is breathtaking. Free entry is a bonus.

pois.name: Whangamomona Hotel & Republic
pois.short: Historic hotel in a self-declared republic on the Forgotten World Highway.
pois.commentary: The Whangamomona Hotel is the social heart of the self-declared Republic of Whangamomona, a tiny settlement on the Forgotten World Highway between Stratford and Taumarunui. The settlement declared independence from New Zealand in 1989 when local government boundary changes placed it in a different district. The republic has its own president (past presidents have included a goat and a poodle) and issues tongue-in-cheek passports to visitors. Republic Day, held every two years in January, draws thousands for quirky competitions and celebrations. The hotel itself is a classic New Zealand country pub — unpretentious, welcoming, and serving hearty meals. The journey to get there, on the winding, remote Forgotten World Highway, is half the experience.

reviews.summary: Quintessential rural New Zealand character. The republic backstory is hilarious. The pub lunch is excellent.

pois.name: Stony River Walk & Ōākura Beach
pois.short: Bohemian coastal village with excellent surf, riverside walks, and artisan cafes.
pois.commentary: Ōākura is a small, bohemian coastal village west of New Plymouth that punches well above its weight for atmosphere and quality. The village has excellent surf breaks, a beautiful sandy beach, and riverside walking tracks along the Ōākura River. The cafe scene is outstanding for a settlement of this size — particularly the famous Ōākura Bay café. The village attracts artists, surfers, and creative types who contribute to its relaxed, alternative vibe. Behind the village, walks along the Ōākura River pass through native bush. The Koru Pa Historic Reserve on the headland between Ōākura and New Plymouth offers panoramic coastal views and well-preserved Maori pa terracing.

reviews.summary: A gem of a village with great surf, lovely walks, and surprisingly good dining. Perfect half-day visit.

pois.name: Tawhiti Museum
pois.short: One of NZ's most innovative museums — life-size dioramas and scale models depicting Taranaki history.
pois.commentary: Tawhiti Museum near Hawera is consistently rated one of the most innovative museums in New Zealand. Created by artist Nigel Ogle, the museum uses life-size figurative dioramas and exquisitely detailed scale models to depict Taranaki's history from early Māori settlement through the Land Wars to colonial farming. The figures are cast from real local people, giving them an uncanny realism. The outdoor Traders & Whalers section features a full-size trading post on the banks of the Tawhiti River, complete with a boat ride through scenes depicting early European contact. The museum is a labour of love, privately funded and constantly expanding, and its approach to history is vivid and engaging.

reviews.summary: Absolutely extraordinary museum that brings history to life. The dioramas are breathtakingly realistic. A national treasure hidden in rural Taranaki.

pois.name: Stratford Shakespeare Garden & Clock Tower
pois.short: Town named after Shakespeare's birthplace with a Tudor-style clock tower that performs Shakespearean scenes.
pois.commentary: Stratford is the only town in New Zealand named after Shakespeare's birthplace, Stratford-upon-Avon. Its Tudor-style clock tower on Broadway performs scenes from Romeo and Juliet at 10am, 1pm, and 3pm — figures emerge and act out scenes to music. The Shakespeare Garden displays plants mentioned in Shakespeare's works. The town's streets are named after Shakespearean characters — Romeo Street, Juliet Street, Hamlet Street. While undeniably quirky, the commitment to the theme is charming and reflects the earnest civic pride of small-town New Zealand. Stratford also serves as a gateway to Mount Taranaki's eastern slopes.

reviews.summary: Charmingly quirky Shakespearean theme. The clock tower show is fun. Worth a brief stop on the way to Mt Taranaki.

pois.name: Rotokare Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Predator-fenced lake sanctuary with thriving kiwi population in rural South Taranaki.
pois.commentary: Rotokare Scenic Reserve is a predator-fenced wildlife sanctuary enclosing a beautiful lake and surrounding bush in rural South Taranaki. The 230-hectare reserve is surrounded by a predator-proof fence, allowing native species to thrive. North Island brown kiwi have been reintroduced and are breeding successfully, alongside kererū, tūī, bellbirds, and fernbirds. The lake itself is excellent for kayaking and swimming. Walking tracks loop through the bush and around the lake shore. Rotokare demonstrates what's possible with community-driven conservation — the reserve was fenced and restored entirely by local volunteer effort. It's a peaceful, uncrowded alternative to larger sanctuaries.

reviews.summary: Beautiful lake and thriving wildlife in a community-run sanctuary. Kiwi numbers are growing. Peaceful and uncrowded.

pois.name: New Plymouth Waterfront & Wind Wand
pois.short: Vibrant waterfront precinct centred on Len Lye's 45-metre kinetic Wind Wand sculpture.
pois.commentary: New Plymouth's waterfront precinct is one of the best-designed urban waterfronts in New Zealand, centred on Len Lye's iconic Wind Wand — a 45-metre carbon fibre tube topped with a glowing red ball that sways gracefully in the wind. The Coastal Walkway stretches 13km along the shore, passing the striking Te Rewa Rewa Bridge (an architectural landmark inspired by a whale skeleton and a breaking wave), public artworks, playgrounds, and swimming spots. The waterfront brings together art, engineering, and landscape in a way that reflects New Plymouth's creative identity. The walkway is popular with walkers, joggers, and cyclists at all hours.

reviews.summary: Outstanding waterfront design with iconic public art. The Coastal Walkway is superb. Te Rewa Rewa Bridge is architecturally stunning.

pois.name: Hollard Gardens
pois.short: Free heritage garden blending exotic plantings with native bush near Kaponga.
pois.commentary: Hollard Gardens near Kaponga is a free, council-managed garden that blends exotic plantings with native bush in a tranquil rural setting. Established by Bernie and Rose Hollard from the 1920s, the garden features rhododendrons, magnolias, camellias, and rare trees alongside regenerating native bush connected by gentle walking paths. The contrast between manicured garden and wild bush — sometimes side by side — creates a unique atmosphere. Spring brings spectacular rhododendron and magnolia flowering. The gardens rarely attract crowds, offering a peaceful experience distinct from the more visited Pukeiti. A small visitor centre has information on the Hollard family's legacy.

reviews.summary: Lovely free garden in a peaceful rural setting. The exotic-native contrast is unusual. Spring flowering is beautiful.

pois.name: Tupare Heritage Garden
pois.short: Heritage Arts and Crafts house with English-style gardens overlooking the Waiwhakaiho River.
pois.commentary: Tupare is a heritage property featuring an English Arts and Crafts-style house set in beautifully maintained gardens above the Waiwhakaiho River in New Plymouth. The house, built in 1927 for Sir Russell and Lady Matthews, is one of New Zealand's finest examples of the Arts and Crafts domestic style. The gardens cascade down to the river in a series of terraces, featuring herbaceous borders, specimen trees, and a productive kitchen garden. Now managed by the Taranaki Regional Council, Tupare is free to visit and offers a glimpse into the gracious lifestyle of early 20th-century New Plymouth society. The river walk extends the visit with native bush and birdlife.

reviews.summary: Beautiful heritage property with lovely gardens. Free entry. The river walk is a bonus. Peaceful and well maintained.

pois.name: Lake Rotokare Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Predator-free sanctuary with thriving native bird populations.
pois.commentary: Lake Rotokare is one of Taranaki's ecological treasures, a 230-hectare scenic reserve surrounded by a predator-proof fence since 2008. The sanctuary protects thriving populations of native birds including kiwi, tīeke (saddleback), pōpokotea (whitehead), and toutouwai (robin). The 1-2 hour Rotokare Loop Track circles the lake through regenerating native bush, passing wetlands alive with birdsong. Dawn and dusk are the best times for birdwatching, and you may hear kiwi calling at night. Managed by the Rotokare Scenic Reserve Trust with community volunteers, it's a powerful example of grassroots conservation in action. Free entry; donations appreciated.

reviews.summary: Birdwatchers love the abundant native species. Visitors appreciate the peaceful lakeside walk and the inspiring community conservation story behind the reserve.

pois.name: Pukekura Park & Fernery
pois.short: Award-winning botanical park with a stunning fernery and display houses.
pois.commentary: Pukekura Park is a 52-hectare botanical garden in the heart of New Plymouth, considered one of New Zealand's premier parks and a Garden of National Significance. The park features two ornamental lakes, a waterfall, walking trails through native bush, and the famous Fernery and Display Houses built into a natural dell. The fernery showcases an extensive collection of native and exotic ferns in a subtropical atmosphere. During the annual TSB Festival of Lights (December-February), the park transforms with illuminated displays and live performances. By day, the park offers peaceful walks, rowboat hire on the main lake, and a playground. Free entry year-round; the Brooklands Zoo is adjacent.

reviews.summary: Locals and visitors alike praise the park's beauty and variety. The Festival of Lights is a highlight, and the fernery is described as magical. Excellent for families.

pois.name: Ōpunakē Clifftop Walkway
pois.short: Scenic clifftop loop with views of dramatic black-sand beach and Mt Taranaki.
pois.commentary: The Ōpunakē Clifftop Walkway is a 2km loop track that follows the cliff edge above the town's dramatic black-sand beach and natural rock pools. The walk offers panoramic views of the Tasman Sea, the volcanic coastline, and on clear days, the towering presence of Mt Taranaki to the north. The track passes through a mix of native coastal scrub and farmland, with several viewpoints and seats along the way. At the beach below, the natural rock pools provide safe swimming at low tide, and the surf break is popular with local surfers. The walkway connects to the town centre, making it easy to combine with a café stop. Free and accessible year-round.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the easy walk with dramatic coastal views. The natural rock pools at the beach are a bonus, and the mountain backdrop on clear days is stunning.

pois.name: Kaupokonui Beach
pois.short: Sheltered black-sand beach with safe swimming and streamside camping.
pois.commentary: Kaupokonui Beach is a family-friendly black-sand beach on Taranaki's south coast, popular with locals for its relatively sheltered swimming conditions. The Kaupokonui Stream enters the sea here, creating a calmer section ideal for children. A basic DOC campsite sits right behind the dunes, offering affordable beachside camping in summer. The beach is a reliable surf break for intermediate surfers, while the stream mouth attracts whitebaiters in season. The surrounding farmland and volcanic coastline create a quintessentially rural Taranaki experience. Located just off Surf Highway 45, it's an excellent stop for a swim and picnic during a coastal road trip.

reviews.summary: Families love the safe swimming area near the stream. The campsite is basic but the beachfront location can't be beaten. A quiet alternative to busier Taranaki beaches.

pois.name: Te Popo Gardens & African Sculpture Park
pois.short: A 13-hectare private garden near Ōakura scattered with 40 life-size sculptures of African animals — delightfully eccentric.
pois.commentary: Te Popo Gardens is the creation of a couple who combined horticultural passion with a love of African wildlife sculpture. Forty life-size bronze and steel elephants, giraffes, lions and rhinos are dotted through 13 hectares of native and exotic plantings, with Mount Taranaki rising behind. Children adore it and adults find it equally charming. Open by appointment and during the Taranaki Garden Festival. The combination of subtropical planting and savannah sculpture against the volcano backdrop is unlike anything else in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe it as wonderfully unexpected. The sculptures are remarkably detailed. Some note limited opening hours require advance planning.

pois.name: Lakeside Players Theatre, Ōpunake
pois.short: A tiny community theatre in coastal Ōpunake staging surprisingly ambitious productions — small-town NZ at its creative best.
pois.commentary: The Lakeside Players have been staging productions in Ōpunake since the 1960s, performing in what was once the town's picture theatre. The programme ranges from local comedies to surprisingly ambitious Shakespeare and contemporary drama, all performed by a dedicated cast of farmers, teachers and townspeople. Check what's on — if your timing aligns, an evening here is a genuinely memorable slice of rural New Zealand cultural life. The theatre also hosts occasional touring acts and film screenings.

reviews.summary: Audiences love the community spirit and surprisingly high production values. It's an authentic small-town experience. Shows sell out quickly in this tight-knit community.

pois.name: Whitecliffs Brewing Company
pois.short: A small-batch craft brewery in Inglewood producing hop-forward ales inspired by Taranaki's landscape.
pois.commentary: Whitecliffs Brewing operates out of a converted shed in Inglewood, producing small-batch ales that reflect the Taranaki terroir. The brewery takes its name from the dramatic white cliff formations along the northern Taranaki coast. Their rotating tap list leans hop-forward with occasional nods to Belgian and English traditions. The tasting room is low-key and welcoming — pull up a stool, work through a tasting paddle, and chat with the brewers. A good pit stop between the mountain and the coast.

reviews.summary: Beer enthusiasts appreciate the quality and variety. The relaxed atmosphere and friendly staff are highlights. Some note it's easy to miss — look for the signs.

pois.name: Kapuni Loop Track
pois.short: A peaceful loop through lush podocarp forest on Taranaki's southern slopes — birdsong, ferns, and solitude.
pois.commentary: The Kapuni Loop Track threads through mature podocarp forest on the lower southern slopes of Mount Taranaki, passing towering rimu, kahikatea, and tree ferns. The gentle 45-minute loop is well-maintained and suitable for families, but feels surprisingly wild — the birdsong here is excellent, with tūī, bellbird, fantail and grey warbler commonly heard. It's far less visited than the northern tracks and offers a quieter, more contemplative forest experience. The access road off Manaia Road is unsealed but manageable in a standard car.

reviews.summary: Walkers appreciate the peace and beautiful forest. The birdsong is exceptional. Some note the access road is rough but passable. A hidden gem away from the crowds.

pois.name: Waverley Beach & Iron Sand Dunes
pois.short: Vast black iron-sand dunes on Taranaki's remote southern coast — wild, windswept, and wonderfully empty.
pois.commentary: Waverley Beach sits at the southern edge of the Taranaki region where the coast becomes increasingly remote and wild. The black iron-sand dunes here are some of the largest in the region, sculpted by relentless Tasman winds into dramatic formations. The beach stretches for kilometres in both directions with barely another soul in sight. Swimming is dangerous due to rips, but the walking and photography are superb. The ironsand has been mined historically and has a distinctive magnetic quality — bring a magnet and watch the sand cling to it.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the wild, remote atmosphere. Photography opportunities are excellent. The beach is not suitable for swimming. Some find the access road bumpy.

pois.name: Hāwera Water Tower & Museum
pois.short: Climb the 1914 concrete water tower for panoramic views over Hāwera and south Taranaki's dairy country.
pois.commentary: The Hāwera Water Tower was built in 1914 and stands 54 metres tall — one of the few heritage water towers in New Zealand that visitors can climb. The 215 steps spiral up through the interior to an observation platform with sweeping views over the dairy plains of south Taranaki, from Mount Taranaki to the coast. At the base, the attached KiwiNorth Museum houses local history exhibits including dairy heritage and the story of the Taranaki wars. It's a quick but rewarding stop that most travellers drive straight past.

reviews.summary: The views from the top are worth the climb. The tower is a unique piece of heritage. Some find the spiral staircase tiring. The museum adds context to the climb.

pois.name: Ōaonui Beach Fur Seal Haul-Out
pois.short: A rocky stretch of coast near Ōaonui where New Zealand fur seals haul out to rest — a quiet, free wildlife encounter.
pois.commentary: The rocky platforms near Ōaonui on Surf Highway 45 are a regular haul-out spot for New Zealand fur seals, particularly in winter and spring. Unlike more famous seal colonies, this spot sees few visitors and offers a chance to observe the animals undisturbed from a respectful distance. Keep at least 10 metres back and never get between a seal and the sea. The coast here is dramatic — black rock, white surf, and the ever-present cone of Taranaki behind. Check the rocks carefully, as the seals blend in remarkably well.

reviews.summary: A pleasant surprise for those driving the Surf Highway. Numbers vary seasonally. Some visitors report not seeing any seals — timing matters. Free and uncrowded.

pois.name: York Street Creative Precinct, New Plymouth
pois.short: A cluster of independent galleries, studios and shops on New Plymouth's York Street — the creative heart of Taranaki.
pois.commentary: York Street and its surrounds form the creative quarter of New Plymouth, housing independent galleries, artist studios, vintage shops and speciality cafés. The precinct has a strong community of local artists working in glass, ceramics, painting and textile art, many of whom open their studios to visitors. First Friday evening events bring live music, late openings and a festive atmosphere. It's a great place to find unique Taranaki-made souvenirs and support independent makers. The energy is relaxed and welcoming — New Plymouth's artsy side at its most accessible.

reviews.summary: A pleasant area to explore with some excellent galleries. First Friday events are popular. The shops are varied and interesting. A good complement to the Len Lye Centre and Govett-Brewster.

pois.name: Egmont Road Forest Walk (Goblin Forest)
pois.short: A mossy, epiphyte-draped 'goblin forest' on Mount Taranaki's lower slopes — ethereal and otherworldly.
pois.commentary: The goblin forests on Mount Taranaki's lower slopes are among the most atmospheric landscapes in New Zealand. The Egmont Road track enters a world where every branch and trunk is sheathed in thick moss, hung with filmy ferns and dripping with moisture. The trees are stunted and contorted by altitude and wind, creating an otherworldly canopy. In fog — which is frequent — the forest becomes genuinely eerie. Several short loops are accessible from Egmont Road, making this an easy half-day walk that feels like stepping into a fantasy landscape. The forest is also home to rifleman and whitehead.

reviews.summary: One of the most atmospheric forest walks in New Zealand. Best in misty conditions for the full effect. Short and accessible. Photography opportunities are exceptional.

pois.name: Lake Rotokare Kiwi Night Walk
pois.short: Listen for wild kiwi calls on a guided night walk around a predator-fenced lake sanctuary in south Taranaki.
pois.commentary: Lake Rotokare Scenic Reserve is a community-led conservation triumph — a predator-fenced sanctuary surrounding a bush-fringed lake near Eltham. The reserve has reintroduced North Island brown kiwi, and guided night walks offer an excellent chance of hearing their piercing calls echoing through the forest. The lake loop walk takes about an hour and passes through some of the finest native bush in the region. Daytime visits are equally rewarding — the birdsong is extraordinary, with tūī, bellbird, kererū, and fantail all abundant. The project is entirely community-funded and volunteer-run, making a visit both a wildlife experience and a celebration of grassroots conservation.

reviews.summary: A wonderful community conservation project. Night walks for kiwi are popular — book ahead. The daytime bush walk is equally rewarding. The lake setting is peaceful. A heartening conservation success story.

pois.name: Stratford Heritage Trail
pois.short: Heritage walking trail through New Zealand's Shakespeare-themed town.
pois.commentary: Stratford's streets are named after Shakespearean characters and the town features a working glockenspiel clock tower. The heritage walkway passes the Malone Memorial Gates, pioneer cemetery, and several historic buildings telling the story of early Taranaki settlement.

reviews.summary: Charming small-town heritage walk with unique Shakespeare theme.

pois.name: Lucy's Gully Loop Walk
pois.short: Enchanting loop walk through moss-draped goblin forest on Mount Taranaki's lower slopes.
pois.commentary: This family-friendly loop track winds through dense kamahi and rimu forest draped in mosses and ferns, creating an eerie 'goblin forest' atmosphere. The accessible track is one of Taranaki's best short walks, especially atmospheric on misty days.

reviews.summary: Magical goblin forest walk, short and sweet.

pois.name: Mike Ally Walkway — Te Henui
pois.short: Urban walkway following Te Henui Stream through native bush and historic sites.
pois.commentary: This peaceful walkway follows Te Henui Stream from the city centre through regenerating native bush. Along the way it passes heritage features including old stone walls and the Te Henui Cemetery, one of Taranaki's oldest European burial grounds.

reviews.summary: Surprisingly lush urban walk with interesting heritage features.

pois.name: Ngāmotu Beach Snorkelling
pois.short: Sheltered snorkelling beach with rich marine life near the Sugar Loaf Islands.
pois.commentary: Ngāmotu Beach sits in the lee of the Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Protected Area, offering calm waters with abundant marine life. The rocky reef supports colourful fish, octopus, and crayfish — one of Taranaki's best kept aquatic secrets.

reviews.summary: Great snorkelling spot, calm and full of marine life.

pois.name: Dawson Falls — Kapuni Loop Track
pois.short: Scenic loop walk through montane forest to a beautiful 18-metre waterfall.
pois.commentary: Starting from the Dawson Falls Visitor Centre on Mount Taranaki's southeastern slopes, the Kapuni Loop passes through stunning goblin forest to reach Dawson Falls, an 18-metre cascade surrounded by moss-laden trees. Several side tracks extend the adventure into the alpine zone.

reviews.summary: Beautiful waterfall walk in atmospheric montane forest.

pois.name: Ōkato Coastal Art Trail
pois.short: Rural art trail featuring sculptures and installations along the Ōkato coastline.
pois.commentary: This self-drive art trail showcases works by local and national artists installed along the rugged Ōkato coastline. Pieces range from large-scale steel sculptures to subtle environmental installations, set against the dramatic backdrop of farmland and surf coast.

reviews.summary: Quirky art trail with ocean views — a creative detour.

pois.name: Pātea Dam Kayak Trail
pois.short: Paddle the scenic Pātea Dam lake surrounded by farmland and native bush.
pois.commentary: Lake Rotorangi behind the Pātea Dam stretches 46 kilometres through remote Taranaki valleys. It's one of the longest artificial lakes in New Zealand, offering multi-day kayak adventures through peaceful countryside with abundant birdlife including shag colonies and marsh birds.

reviews.summary: Peaceful paddling through beautiful remote valleys.

pois.name: Wilkies Pools
pois.short: A short Dawson Falls walk to smooth lava pools carved by Kapuni Stream on the slopes of Taranaki Maunga.
pois.commentary: Wilkies Pools is a classic Taranaki short walk: quick enough for families, memorable enough to linger. Starting near Dawson Falls Visitor Centre, the DOC loop passes through dense kāmahi forest to the Kapuni Stream, where water has scoured smooth pools into old lava. The first section to the pools is the easiest; continuing the full loop involves stream-edge care and conditions can change quickly on the maunga. The pools are best treated as a place to look, cool your hands and listen, not a place to underestimate. Heavy rain can make the stream crossings and banks unsafe.

reviews.summary: Visitors praise the short forest approach and sculpted lava pools; weather, stream levels and road-end conditions are the main cautions.

pois.name: Te Henui Walkway
pois.short: A leafy New Plymouth stream walkway linking neighbourhood parks with the coastal path.
pois.commentary: Te Henui Walkway is everyday New Plymouth at its best: stream shade, bridges, birds and a clean link down to the coast. It is not dramatic, but it makes the city feel walkable, green and quietly well-loved.

pois.name: Taranaki Pioneer Village
pois.short: An open-air heritage village near Stratford, preserving buildings and everyday objects from earlier rural life.
pois.commentary: Taranaki Pioneer Village gathers small-town history at human scale. Shops, cottages, tools and vehicles create a walk-through version of the region’s settler-era life, useful for anyone who likes history with doors, handles and weatherboards.

pois.name: Ōpunake Loop Trail
pois.short: A compact town-and-coast loop linking Ōpunake Beach, lake edges, murals and Surf Highway scenery.
pois.commentary: Ōpunake Loop Trail turns a beach town into a rounded stop rather than a fuel break. The route links surf, lake, streets and coastal viewpoints, showing how much character is packed into this small western Taranaki settlement.

pois.name: Pātea River Historic Walk
pois.short: A South Taranaki river-town walk connecting port history, public art and local memory.
pois.commentary: Pātea’s river walk is a modest but worthwhile way to see a town shaped by water, industry and change. It pairs well with the town’s music and museum stories, giving travellers a reason to slow down on the coast road.

pois.name: Puke Ariki to Len Lye Centre Walk
pois.short: A central New Plymouth culture pairing: regional museum stories and Len Lye’s kinetic-art shine.
pois.commentary: Puke Ariki and the Len Lye Centre make New Plymouth’s compact centre unusually rich. One grounds you in Taranaki history and collections; the other throws light, movement and stainless steel into the day, turning a short walk into a cultural double feature.

pois.short: St Mary’s is one of New Plymouth’s oldest stone landmarks, carrying colonial, community and conflict-era memory in the heart of the city.

pois.name: Taranaki Aviation Technology and Transport Museum
pois.short: A hands-on roadside museum south of New Plymouth, preserving aviation, transport and early technology.
pois.commentary: Taranaki Aviation Technology and Transport Museum is wonderfully mechanical. Aircraft, vehicles, radios, printing gear and working exhibits turn regional history into switches, engines and machines, making it a strong rainy-day stop for curious travellers.
pois.audio_story: Taranaki Aviation Technology and Transport Museum is wonderfully mechanical. Aircraft, vehicles, radios, printing gear and working exhibits turn regional history into switches, engines and machines, making it a strong rainy-day stop for curious travellers.

pois.name: Lucy's Gully
pois.short: A forested picnic and track access point in the Kaitake Range, just inland from Oakura.
pois.commentary: Lucy's Gully is a cool green Taranaki pause after Surf Highway salt and sun. Redwoods, native bush and access to longer Kaitake Range tracks make it useful for a picnic, a short wander, or the start of a more ambitious climb toward Patuhā Trig.

pois.name: Pukekura Railway
pois.short: A mapped local attraction that helps fill in the visitor map for Taranaki.
pois.commentary: Pukekura Railway gives Taranaki another indoor, human-scale stop: a local attraction where the trip shifts from scenery to the people, collections and performances that shape the district. OpenStreetMap describes it as Miniture railway clubrooms and track. It is useful on wet-weather days, slower town walks, or any itinerary that needs more than lookouts and beaches.
